title: Cat Swallowed String or Ribbon? Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat swallowed string, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about linear foreign bodies and why you must never pull the string yourself.
content_html:
TL;DR: A linear foreign body like string or yarn is a life-threatening emergency that can cause severe intestinal damage. If your cat swallows string, do not pull it and contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

What is a linear foreign body in cats and why is it dangerous?

When a cat ingests a long, thin item such as sewing thread, yarn, holiday ribbon, or dental floss, it is referred to as a linear foreign body. These objects are particularly dangerous because they tend to become anchored at one point—most commonly under the tongue or at the exit of the stomach. As the rest of the string moves through the digestive tract, the intestines begin to bunch up like an accordion, which can lead to severe tissue damage or life-threatening perforations.

How urgent is it if my cat swallowed a piece of string or yarn?

  • Treat swallowing string as a high-priority veterinary emergency, as internal damage can be progressive and silent.
  • Do not pull any string protruding from the cat’s mouth or rectum, as this can act like a saw and slice through the intestinal walls.
  • Pulling the material can cause fatal abdominal infections and severe tissue trauma.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately for professional assessment.

Why should I take a photo of the swallowed material for the veterinarian?

  • Take a clear photo of any leftover material next to a ruler or common object for scale.
  • The photo helps the medical team estimate the length, thickness, and material of the swallowed object.
  • This information is critical for determining if the vet can induce vomiting, attempt endoscopic retrieval, or if the cat requires immediate emergency surgery.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, linear foreign bodies such as string, yarn, or dental floss are commonly ingested and can be particularly challenging due to being non-palpable, making length assessment difficult. Linear foreign bodies are more likely to cause gastrointestinal mucosal damage, devitalization, and can affect a large section of the GI tract, potentially leading to obstruction or perforation. Exploratory laparotomy is indicated if the foreign body is unable to pass through the GI tract. Devitalized or perforated areas of the GI tract must be resected and anastomosed. Multiple enterotomies may be indicated, but the minimal number possible to remove the foreign body is recommended to decrease the risk of postoperative dehiscence.

Chapter: Surgery, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 392)

faqs.question: What are the signs that the string is causing a problem?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include repeated vomiting, loss of appetite, lethargy, diarrhea, and signs of abdominal pain such as hiding or hunching over.

faqs.question: Can a cat pass a long string on its own?
faqs.answer: While small pieces might pass, long strings are highly likely to become entangled. Because the risk of intestinal rupture is so high, you should never 'wait and see' with linear foreign bodies.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to help my cat?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely perform a physical exam (including checking under the tongue), followed by X-rays or an ultrasound to look for characteristic bunching of the intestines.
